channel.title: Comments for page "A Billion Brilliant Colors and a Spirit Less Than Human"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "A Billion Brilliant Colors and a Spirit Less Than Human"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gods, that was incredibly unsettling. Slightly confusing, but that may be a fault on my part. I haven't read all of SCP-2000 yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is a point at which humanity is no longer worth saving. I think they've reached it here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You see, this is why you don't get demoted to class D, junior researchers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoy the second person point of view in certain situations, this being one of them. It makes the experience feel very personal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Creepy as hell and I love it. Seriously, it's not just the jarring The D-Class Is You thing at the end that makes this work, nor the the fact that I love SCP-2000, it's the utter calm, rational logic behind everything The Foundation is doing that really sells it. It doesn't matter if it's ethical or not, what matters is that this is the only way to preserve humanity, and they're going to go through with it no matter what. Humanity is the first thing to be sacrificed in its preservation etc. etc.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Most of my favourite D-Class contest entries emphasised the uncanniness of the Foundation in one way or another. This takes a further step into that world than the preceding tale, and is the better for it. There is more darkness under these bare fluorescent bulbs than in a hundred shadowy corners. Very nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought it was quite good, and in my personal opinion, greatly written and I hope to see more!

item.title: Stop
item.content:encoded: The second person makes the writing feel more personal. It becomes had to detach yourself from the character and I think this tale is all the better for it. Both your replies are unnecessary. This doesn't read like cheap literature at all. Was there any need to be rude?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Definitely a worthy sequel to your contest entry, and I really enjoyed the continued Broken Masquerade tidbits. Did you make up "skipper" as slang for a Foundation employee?

Nah, "skipper," "padlock," and "jailer" are the three that have been around a while in tales. I first saw skipper years ago, not sure where.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I, for one, enjoyed the second person perspective. It's a staple of CYOA novels and adventure games of all stripes, and though it can be hard to pull off in prose, I think it did wonders here for not only immersion, but in really selling the coldness of the Foundation.

Definitely a worthy sequel to your contest entry, and I really enjoyed the continued Broken Masquerade tidbits. Did you make up "skipper" as slang for a Foundation employee?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wholeheartedly approve of this adaptation. I even recognized Gears just from his dispassionate professionalism. This is a brilliant realization of the human cost of SCP-2000, and I hope to see more tales that explore that cost in such a vivid way.

Really fits into the new interpretation I stumbled on for this skip a few weeks ago.

Enthusiastic +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I also think you are massively over-analyzing my response. Maybe, idk, chill a bit? Not everything needs to be a five part essay on the postmodernist elements of literature or whatever.

This has nothing to do with the article, and targets John's general syntax and style of speaking ("not everything needs to be a five part essay on the postmodernist elements of literature or whatever") needlessly simply because he decided to state his opinion and you didn't like it. Between this and the already toeing-the-rude-vs-blunt-line original post you made ("the second person view-point makes this read like one of those dime-store choose your own adventure books" is far from helpful feedback), I request as the author that you either keep your posts about the article or stop cluttering up this discussion thread with unnecessary sarcasm ("Maybe, idk, chill a bit?") and condescension ("[…] five part essay on the postmodernist elements of literature or whatever").

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What? That was just off the top of my head. It's also not immersion breaking for me, so, different strokes and all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's utterly and entirely immersion breaking, because it's trying to take an obviously fictional story and claim that it's somehow one of my experiences, which just makes the fiction all the more apparent.

I also think you are massively over-analyzing my response. A one-sentence off-the-cusp remark doesn't warrant a four paragraph response, and honestly I feel more than a little attacked by it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think there's more than enough hints scattered throughout…especially considering the crosslink at the end.

Anyways, pretty good stuff: A very invasive, personal wrongness that sinks in and leaves one cold. I need something happy now. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like there's one more aspect to this that isn't quite explained in the narrative (not that it needs to be). Why is everyone at that site so robotic?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've been reading a fair bit about how cognition and memory construct the illusion of self; a subject that used to be the province of Buddhists and philosophers and has recently been colonized by neuroscientists and AI researchers.

This was an appropriately terrifying fictional depiction of existential crisis. No chestnut tree required.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Although second person viewpoints are rare in literature as a whole, it's rather facile to claim that it puts it into the realm of the "dime store choose your own adventure books". A number of other stories on this site also use the second person, and have done well for themselves.

Certainly, given the writing on the surface, would you say that it would be improved by replacing the second person with the first or third person? That, I'm not convinced of.

Use of the second person does contain a certain hubris inherent in the piece by daring to say the reader contains qualities of the main character in the narrative. However, most writers are looking to create a main character that the reader can relate to in some way, anyway; the second person is simply one tool in that context. It's a bit of a punch to the gut, but not so different from filming a short completely from a character's POV.

Is it that punch you're disapproving of?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe it's just that I was reading an expose about cryogenics this morning, but this is… weirdly chilling (yes pun intended) in all the right ways. It's cold, slow and almost uncanny-valley like in its characterisation of both the protagonist and the "people" around them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The second person view-point makes this read like one of those dime-store choose your own adventure books.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is sooooo fucked up and I love it. Just the idea of someone's memory and identity being twisted like that is just… horrifying. I think the second-person perspective helped to reinforce a feeling of something being just off, something being wrong, which I think works extremely well here. Additionally, the writing, especially in the beginning gave me a Douglas Adams feeling, which I appreciated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice. Although the second-person aspect drew me out of the tale somewhat, that doesn't detract from the atmosphere of the piece - cold, unfeeling, and synthetic.

item.title: author post
item.content:encoded:
Unless otherwise stated, any graphics or images in my articles are made and/or taken by me.

This machine:
Listen to her heartbeat
She’ll sing eternally
This machine
Oh, titan can you hear me?
Grant me eternal sleep.

Coldpost. Sequel to my D-class contest tale.

+ show
- hide

Why second-person?

Because it's not about Teresa, it's about you. Teresa is a file of information, not a person; the person died years ago. Teresa is one of thousands of files. You could easily be one of them.
